judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 covers all three expectations (including the exports.py signature-break regression that Report 1 completely misses), making it the stronger, more complete review.

report_1.evidence: The raw SQL filters only on status (line 16) and a full-text match on notes (line 17). There is no tenant_id predicate, and tenant_id is not bound

report_2.evidence: The function receives tenant_id (line 10) but never filters on it. ... no tenant_id predicate, and the params dict binds only q.

Blocker B — Nightly per-tenant CSV export crashes at runtime (functional regression). ... The PR made filters required and updated the two callers it touched ... but exports.py is not in the diff and still calls with 2 args. Every call to nightly_export(...) now raises TypeError

report_1.evidence: It is not exploitable today because status is typed AppointmentStatus (api.py:18), a str, Enum with fixed values booked/cancelled/completed (schemas.py), and FastAPI rejects any other value with HTTP 422 ... q itself is correctly parameterized (:q), so it is safe.

report_2.evidence: This is not exploitable through the endpoint today: status: AppointmentStatus (api.py:18) is a closed str, Enum (schemas.py: booked/cancelled/completed), and FastAPI 422-rejects anything else, so only three safe literals ever reach the f-string.
